chip_catalogue.effect: A composition row names the raga (Kapi), the tala, and the composer — Dharmapuri Subbaraya Iyer, who wrote songs directly for Veenai Dhanammal herself. Tapping the raga opens its complete Bani Flow trail in the right panel.

Begada is a rakti raga — a mode of deep emotional saturation — central to the Dhanammal bani. Ramnad Krishnan's rendering of Begada is said to be unparalleled. Tapping a raga chip opens its Bani Flow trail: every musician in the graph who has recorded in that raga.

A concert row opens a scrollable setlist — every piece in that evening's programme, playable in sequence. Ramnad Krishnan at Poonamallee (1965): student of T. Brinda, carrying the Dhanammal bani in full flight. ▶ opens the player; ↗ opens YouTube.

demo_row.play_label: Manodharma — A Lec-Dem Part One (TM Krishna)

chip_catalogue.effect: Lec-dem rows appear at the top of a musician's profile. TM Krishna's Manodharma — three lectures on improvisation — is the finest available introduction to how a performer thinks inside a raga: where the freedom is, what it is constrained by, and why it sounds the way it does. Begin here.

demo_row.play_label: Manodharma — A Lec-Dem Part Two (TM Krishna)

chip_catalogue.effect: Part Two: how alapana gives way to the composed kriti, and how kalpana swara — improvised melodic phrases on solmisation syllables — are woven into the performance structure.

demo_row.play_label: Manodharma — A Lec-Dem Part Three (TM Krishna)

chip_catalogue.effect: Part Three: the accompanying musicians — violinist and mridangist — and the dialogic improvisation (thani avartanam, svarakalpana) that makes Carnatic music a conversation, not a recitation.

cross_panel_seeds.prompt: The Dhanammal bani lives most vividly in its signature ragas. Open their trail — and see who in the lineage has sung them:

items.label: Sahana

view_section.label: The tradition lives in two forms:
view_section.graph_note: Guru-Shishya — each node a musician, each edge the teaching relationship through which music is transmitted in the aural-oral tradition
view_section.raga_note: Mela-Janya — the modal world of Carnatic music: 72 parent scales give rise to ragas, but it is the composers
view_section.raga_note_suffix: who gave each raga its individual character through thousands of kritis composed over a lifetime of devotion
view_section.wheel_note: In Mela-Janya view, tapping a raga also illuminates it on the 72-mela wheel — you can see which parent mela governs this raga and navigate to its siblings.

chip_catalogue.effect: A musician chip loads their Bani Flow trail — recordings, lecdems, compositions — into this (right) panel. The Guru-Shishya graph on the left highlights their lineage connections. Madurai Mani Iyer (1912–1968): Sangeetha Kalanidhi 1959, one of the golden age's most fully realised vocal voices — celebrated for kalpana swarams, neraval, and alapana of rare lucidity.

A composition row carries its raga (Mukhari), tala (Adi), and composer (Tyagaraja) — all clickable. A composition is the structured vessel through which a raga's personality becomes audible; each musician's rendering reveals a different face of the same mode.

Gamaka — the ornamental inflection of a swara — is what makes Carnatic music sound the way it does. Without gamaka, a raga is a scale; with it, a single note becomes an emotion. Lakshmi Sreeram's lecture series covers the grammar of gamaka from first principles: this is where good listening begins.

demo_row.play_label: Muripemu Kalige · Mukhari — TM Krishna, Parallel Lines Bangalore 2020
Understanding Raga - Part I — Lakshmi Sreeram

chip_catalogue.effect: The raga chips on a lec-dem are interactive — tapping them focuses that raga on the Mela-Janya wheel. A raga heard and explained in a lecture becomes a navigable node in the modal world. Lakshmi Sreeram's lecture on raga is the clearest available account of what a raga is — not a scale, but a personality; not a set of notes, but a way of moving between them.
